text: The surgeon begins dissection along the greater curvature of the stomach to access the lesser sac, carefully removing omental and peritoneal adhesions from the liver and gallbladder. The dissection proceeds through the hepatocolic ligament to mobilize the hepatic flexure, followed by cocorization and medial reflection of the duodenum. Superior duodenal attachments to the ligament of Treitz are removed, and retrogastric adhesions are meticulously dissected. The gastroepiploic vein is isolated and divided using Weck clips, and dissection continues through the hepatoduodenal ligament.

The surgeon creates a retroduodenal tunnel and transects the duodenum using a 60mm linear stapler. The gastroepiploic arcade is divided with a vascular load, and the gastroduodenal artery is isolated and clipped with Weck clips. Dissection continues to skeletonize the common hepatic duct, which is encircled with umbilical tape. The gallbladder is freed using a top-down approach, and the pancreas is divided at the neck using hook cautery.

The surgeon further divides the ligament of Treitz, retracting the jejunum into the retroportal space. The uncinate process is dissected free from the superior mesenteric artery and vein using a vessel sealer. The common hepatic duct is sharply divided, and the proximal jejunum is transected with a sixty millimeter linear stapler. The pancreatic duct is cannulated with an eight French feeding tube, followed by an end-to-side pancreaticojejunostomy using a three O posterior and anterior layer with a five O ductum mucosa inner layer.

The surgeon performs an end-to-side hepatico-jejunostomy using a single interrupted 4-0 PDS suture layer. The jejunum is positioned in an anticholic fashion, followed by an end-to-side duodenal jejunostomy using a running 3-0 suture layer.
Two 19 French Blake drains are placed, one near the hepatico-jejunostomy and the other near the duodenal jejunostomy. The assist port is slightly upsized to facilitate specimen removal.
